title: Passé Composé vs Imparfait
description: The key A2 distinction: passé composé for single completed events, imparfait for background, ongoing states, and habits. Explained in English with clear signal-word guidance.

cards.back: a single, completed event ('and then X happened')
cards.notes: Use the **passé composé** for anything that happened once and is finished: *Il **a appelé**.* (He called.) *Soudain, elle **est tombée**.* (Suddenly, she fell.) English equivalents: 'did X' or 'has done X' as a single event.

cards.back: background, ongoing state, or habit in the past
cards.notes: Use the **imparfait** for: what was happening (background), what used to happen (habit), and descriptions. *Il **pleuvait**.* (It was raining.) *Elle **travaillait** tous les jours.* (She worked every day.) English: 'was doing', 'used to do'.

cards.front: Je dormais quand le téléphone a sonné.
cards.back: I was sleeping (background) when the phone rang (event).
cards.notes: The classic combination: **imparfait** sets the scene, **passé composé** fires the event that interrupts it. The pattern is: *[imparfait] quand/lorsque [passé composé]*.

cards.front: soudain, tout à coup, un jour, hier, ce jour-là
cards.back: signal words that trigger the passé composé
cards.notes:
These adverbs mark a **specific, punctual moment** — exactly what the passé composé expresses:

| Signal word | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **soudain** | suddenly |
| **tout à coup** | all of a sudden |
| **un jour** | one day |
| **hier** | yesterday |
| **ce jour-là** | that day |
cards.hint: If you can replace the signal with 'suddenly' or 'one day', use passé composé.

cards.front: d'habitude, tous les jours, souvent, toujours, pendant que, quand j'étais
cards.back: signal words that trigger the imparfait
cards.notes:
These expressions mark **repeated, ongoing, or habitual** past action:

| Signal word | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **d'habitude** | usually |
| **tous les jours** | every day |
| **souvent** | often |
| **toujours** | always |
| **pendant que** | while |
| **quand j'étais** | when I was |
cards.hint: If the action repeated or was ongoing, use imparfait.

cards.front: nous parlons → je parlais, tu parlais, il parlait…
cards.notes: Take the **nous** present-tense stem (drop *-ons*), then add the imparfait endings. *parler*: nous parl**ons** → je parl**ais**. *finir*: nous finiss**ons** → je finiss**ais**. Only **être** is irregular: stem *ét-* → j'étais.

cards.front: D'habitude il prenait le bus. Un jour, il a pris un taxi.
cards.back: He usually took the bus. One day, he took a taxi.
cards.notes: *D'habitude* → **imparfait** (habit). *Un jour* → **passé composé** (single event that broke the habit). Both sentences about the same verb *prendre*, but different tenses because the meaning changes.

cards.front: Pendant qu'il lisait, elle a téléphoné.
cards.back: While he was reading, she phoned.
cards.notes: **Pendant que** (while) + imparfait sets the continuous background. The interrupting event gets the passé composé. Compare English: 'was reading' (ongoing) vs 'phoned' (event).

cards.front: Il faisait beau. Les oiseaux chantaient.
cards.back: The weather was nice. The birds were singing.
cards.notes: Pure **description** in the past always uses the **imparfait**: weather, physical appearance, mood, setting the scene. No single event is happening — the camera is just panning over the scene.

cards.front: English 'was doing' → imparfait; 'did / has done' (single event) → passé composé
cards.back: use English tense as a first clue, then check for signal words
cards.notes: This works well as a **first filter** but is not perfect — English 'I used to do' and 'I was doing' both → imparfait. When in doubt, ask: *is this a one-off completed event or a state/habit/background?*

cards.back: Suddenly, he shouted. (passé composé — punctual event)
cards.notes: *Soudain* (suddenly) virtually always triggers the **passé composé** — it marks a sharp, unexpected single moment.

cards.front: Il chantait toujours sous la douche.
cards.back: He always sang in the shower. (imparfait — habit)
cards.notes: *Toujours* (always) here marks a **repeated past habit** — the action happened regularly, so we use the imparfait. (If *toujours* means 'still' in the present, that is a different use.)

steps.title: The core distinction
steps.body:
# Passé Composé vs Imparfait

Both tenses talk about the past. The difference is **not** how far back — it is the **nature** of the action.

| Tense | Use | English clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Passé composé** | Single completed event, 'and then X happened' | 'did X', 'has done X' (once) |
| **Imparfait** | Background, ongoing state, repeated habit | 'was doing X', 'used to do X' |

Think of it as a **movie**: the imparfait is the continuous background footage (the scenery, the weather, what everyone was doing). The passé composé is every **cut** — a new event in the plot.

> *Il **faisait** beau* (imparfait — background: it was a nice day) *quand elle **est arrivée*** (passé composé — event: she arrived).

## The classic pattern

**Imparfait** (what was going on) + **quand/lorsque** + **passé composé** (what happened):

> *Je **dormais** quand le téléphone **a sonné**.* — I was sleeping when the phone rang.

steps.title: Signal words
steps.body:
## Signal words — let the adverb guide you

Certain adverbs and expressions almost always pair with one tense:

### Passé composé signals

| Word / phrase | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **soudain** | suddenly |
| **tout à coup** | all of a sudden |
| **un jour** | one day |
| **hier** | yesterday |
| **ce jour-là** | that day |
| **à ce moment-là** | at that moment |

### Imparfait signals

| Word / phrase | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **d'habitude** | usually |
| **tous les jours** | every day |
| **souvent** | often |
| **toujours** | always |
| **pendant que** | while |
| **quand j'étais enfant** | when I was a child |
| **autrefois** | in the old days |

> **Tip:** If you can substitute the signal with 'suddenly' or 'one day', use passé composé. If you can substitute with 'usually' or 'every day', use imparfait.

steps.title: Forming the imparfait
steps.body:
## How to form the imparfait

Take the **nous** form of the present tense, drop **-ons**, then add these endings:

| Person | Ending | parler example |
|---|---|---|
| je | **-ais** | je parlais |
| tu | **-ais** | tu parlais |
| il/elle | **-ait** | il parlait |
| nous | **-ions** | nous parlions |
| vous | **-iez** | vous parliez |
| ils/elles | **-aient** | ils parlaient |

The only irregular stem is **être**: *j'**étais**, tu **étais**, il **était**…*

## Quick English-speaker guide

| English | French tense |
|---|---|
| *She **called** me yesterday.* (once) | **Passé composé**: elle m'**a appelé** |
| *She **was calling** me.* (ongoing) | **Imparfait**: elle m'**appelait** |
| *She **used to call** me.* (habit) | **Imparfait**: elle m'**appelait** |

> Remember: the imparfait endings **-ais / -ait / -aient** are all pronounced the same. Only the spelling changes.

exercise.prompt: Match each signal word or phrase to the tense it typically triggers.
Translate: *Suddenly, he fell.* (soudain signals a completed event)
exercise.hint: soudain → passé composé; tomber takes être → il est tombé.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *He always sang in the shower.* (habit → imparfait)
accept: Il chantait toujours sous la douche.
il chantait toujours sous la douche
Il chantait toujours sous la douche
exercise.hint: toujours (always) marks a habit → imparfait. chanter: il chantait.
distractors: Il a chanté toujours sous la douche.
Il a toujours chanté sous la douche dans le passé.
Il chante toujours sous la douche.

steps.title: Classic combo: fill the event verb

exercise.prompt: Fill in the passé composé of *sonner* (to ring). Background already set with imparfait.
exercise.sentence: Je dormais quand le téléphone ___ sonné.
exercise.hint: sonner takes avoir; il/le → il a sonné. Fill in the auxiliary 'a'.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the imparfait of *faire* (il fait beau → il ___ beau). Pure description, no event.
exercise.hint: Description of the weather → imparfait. faire: il faisait.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *While he was reading, she phoned.* (pendant que + imparfait, then passé composé)
exercise.hint: pendant que + imparfait (lisait); the phone call = passé composé (a téléphoné).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *Usually she walked, but one day she took a taxi.* Use d'habitude + imparfait, then un jour + passé composé.
accept: D'habitude elle marchait, mais un jour elle a pris un taxi.
d'habitude elle marchait, mais un jour elle a pris un taxi
D'habitude, elle marchait, mais un jour elle a pris un taxi.
D'habitude elle marchait mais un jour elle a pris un taxi.
exercise.hint: d'habitude + marchait (imparfait); un jour + a pris (passé composé, irrégulier: prendre → pris).
distractors: D'habitude elle a marché, mais un jour elle prenait un taxi.
D'habitude elle marchait, mais un jour elle prenait un taxi.
D'habitude elle marche, mais un jour elle a pris un taxi.

exercise.prompt: The image shows: rain falling as a woman reads inside — a calm, ongoing scene. Which sentence fits?

images.label: Il a plu et elle a lu.

exercise.hint: Ongoing background scene → imparfait for both verbs.
distractors: Il a plu et elle a lu.

steps.title: Match the English clue to the correct French tense

exercise.prompt: Match each English description to the French tense it requires.
Arrange the words: *Suddenly she called me.* (soudain → passé composé)
exercise.hint: soudain → passé composé; appeler takes avoir → elle a appelé.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I was eating when she arrived.* (imparfait + quand + passé composé)
accept: Je mangeais quand elle est arrivée.
je mangeais quand elle est arrivée
Je mangeais quand elle est arrivee.
Je mangeais quand elle est arrivée
exercise.hint: 'was eating' → imparfait (je mangeais); 'arrived' = event → passé composé (elle est arrivée, arriver takes être).
distractors: J'ai mangé quand elle est arrivée.
